title: Ensure Activity Tracker trails are integrated with LogDNA  Logs

input_row.title: Ensure Activity Tracker trails are integrated with LogDNA  Logs
input_row.description: Check whether Activity Tracker trails are integrated with LogDNA Logs.
input_row.rationale: IBM Cloud™ Activity Tracker with LogDNA is a Cloud service that you can use to monitor the activity of your IBM Cloud account. IBM Log Analysis with LogDNA is a service that you can use to analyze and monitor operational logs. Both services offer the same features to manage audit events and logs. However, Activity Tracker trails are not integrated with LogDNA Logs. • The data that is collected and processed by the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service is associated with that location and not visible to the other regions by virtue of this association. • The data that is collected and processed by the IBM Log Analysis with LogDNA service is associated with that location and not visible to the other regions by virtue of this association. • Within a service instance, data is isolated per LogDNA instance within a region. The IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service offers soft isolation for data storage. Data is mixed together in the same data stores and segmented by tags that are associated with each of the records to enforce access control policies. When audit data and logs are archived for long-term storage, archives of your operational logs and auditing events can be archived to the same IBM Cloud Object Storage (COS) location.
input_row.audit: Use the following checklist to ensure Activity Tracker trails are integrated with LogDNA Logs: 1. Ensure that IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA instances are provisioned with a service plan that includes the ability to configure archiving to an external storage service. 2. Ensure that IBM Log Analysis with LogDNA instances are provisioned with a service plan that includes the ability to configure archiving to an external storage service. 3. Ensure that you have access to storage, for example, check that you have an instance of the IBM Cloud Object Storage service provisioned in your account. 4. Ensure that you have 1 COS bucket available to archive your data. Check the bucket configuration to verify that it meets your corporate and external market regulations. 5. Ensure that each audit instance has archiving enabled and configured to the bucket identified in step 4. 6. Ensure that each logging instance has archiving enabled and configured to the bucket identified in step 4. To check if audit and logging instances are provisioned and configured in the account, complete the following steps: Note: To complete these steps, your IBM Cloud user ID (IBMid) must have the following roles: • A platform role with viewer role for the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service and the IBM Log Analysis with LogDNA service for the account • A service role with reader role for the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service and the IBM Log Analysis with LogDNA service for the account • A platform role with viewer role for the IBM Cloud Object Storage service for the account • Identity and Access Management (IAM) permissions to view access groups in the account Console: 1. Log in to IBM Cloud at https://cloud.ibm.com. 2. Go to the Menu icon. Then, select Observability to access the Observability dashboard. 3. Select Activity Tracker from the page navigation menu to see the auditing instances. Select Logging from the page navigation menu to see the logging instances. 4. Check that you can see an Activity Tracker instance in Frankfurt and one instance for each location where you operate in the IBM Cloud. 5. To launch the LogDNA web UI, for each instance, select View LogDNA. 6. In the LogDNA web UI, verify that you can see audit events. 7. Identify the LogDNA instance ID in the LogDNA URL. This ID is used as part of the name of the archive file. CLI instructions: 1. Log in to IBM Cloud. Run the following command: ibmcloud login -a cloud.ibm.com For a federated account, run the following command: ibmcloud login -a cloud.ibm.com --sso 2. List all the audit instances that are provisioned in your account. Run the following command: ibmcloud resource service-instances --service-name logdnaat --all- resource-groups List all the logging instances that are provisioned in your account. Run the following command: ibmcloud resource service-instances --service-name logdna --all- resource-groups 3. For Activity Tracker, check that you have one instance in Frankfurt, and one instance for each location where you operate in the IBM Cloud. 4. Get the LogDNA instance ID. This value is used as part of the archive file name. Run the following command: ibmcloud resource service-instance INSTANCE_NAME --output JSON | grep dashboard_url To check the COS instance and bucket in the account where logs and events are archived, complete the following steps: 1. Identify the CRN of the COS instance where archived data must be located. Run the following command to get the list of instances that are available in the account and their CRNs: ibmcloud resource service-instances --type service_instance --service- name cloud-object-storage --all-resource-groups --output JSON 2. Identify the bucket. Run the following command to list all the buckets that are available in the COS instance: ic cos list-buckets --ibm-service-instance-id 3. For each LogDNA instance, identify the corresponding archive files for that instance. Run the following command: ic cos list-objects --bucket --region Note: You must see archive files that include the LogDNA instance ID. If you cannot see any files for that instance, then that instance is not being archived to the bucket. To verify that archiving is enabled in a LogDNA instance, complete the following steps: Note: To complete these steps, your IBM Cloud user ID (IBMid) needs the following roles in addition to the ones specified earlier: • A platform role with operator role for the IBM Cloud Object Storage service for the account • A service role with manager role for the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service and the IBM Log Analysis with LogDNA service for the account, so that you can verify that archiving is configured Console: 1. Log in to IBM Cloud at https://cloud.ibm.com. 2. Go to the Menu icon. Then, select Observability to access the Observability dashboard. 3. Select Activity Tracker or Logging from the page navigation menu 4. For each instance, check that archiving is configured for each audit instance. o To launch the LogDNA web UI, select View LogDNA. o In the LogDNA web UI, select Settings. Then, select Archiving. o Verify that archiving is configured for the audit instance. o Check the bucket name. o Check the endpoint value to make sure that the private endpoints are configured. To verify the correct value, you must check the bucket configuration in IBM Cloud Object Storage. o Check the permissions granted to the API key, limit write access to the audit instance only. To verify the permissions, you must identify the credential (service ID) on which you restricted permissions. o Check that the Cloud Object Storage instance ID is the one where data should be archived. You might have different COS instances to archive data.
input_row.remediation: 1. If the option to configure archiving is not allowed, upgrade the instance’s service plan. 2. If archiving is not enabled for an instance, review and define the archiving strategy. Then, configure archiving for an audit instance. 3. If you cannot find an instance of IBM Cloud Object Storage (COS) in your account, provision one. 4. If you want to create a bucket, see create and configure a bucket.
input_row.impact: Failure to meet this control will prevent you from managing long-term data, logs and audit events, from a common location.
input_row.default_value: By default, archiving of events and logs is not enabled for any LogDNA instance.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control is fully auditable via IBM Cloud APIs and CLI commands, which allow for the verification of the configuration of the Activity Tracker with LogDNA and Log Analysis with LogDNA. 
- The audit steps provided include CLI commands that can be executed to verify the integration of Activity Tracker trails with LogDNA Logs, confirming that all necessary service properties and configurations are accessible programmatically.

**PROGRAM NAME:** ibm_activity_tracker_logdna_integration_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
1. Use IBM Cloud CLI to ensure instances are provisioned:
   - Execute `ibmcloud resource service-instances --service-name logdnaat --all-resource-groups` to list Activity Tracker instances.
   - Execute `ibmcloud resource service-instances --service-name logdna --all-resource-groups` to list Log Analysis instances.

2. Validate the configuration:
   - Ensure that both services are provisioned with appropriate plans permitting archiving.
   - Execute `ibmcloud resource service-instance INSTANCE_NAME --output JSON | grep dashboard_url` to retrieve the LogDNA instance ID for each Activity Tracker.
   
3. Check IBM Cloud Object Storage (COS) configuration:
   - Execute `ibmcloud resource service-instances --type service_instance --service-name cloud-object-storage --all-resource-groups --output JSON` to retrieve the list of COS instances.
   - Execute `ic cos list-buckets --ibm-service-instance-id INSTANCE_ID` to get the list of available buckets.
   
4. Verify archiving settings:
   - For each LogDNA instance, use `ic cos list-objects --bucket BUCKET_NAME --region REGION_NAME` to check for the presence of archive files associated with the LogDNA instance ID.
   - If any LogDNA instance is not archiving properly, it will not return any corresponding archive files, indicating a failure to comply.

5. Criteria for compliance:
   - All required instances (Activity Tracker with LogDNA and Log Analysis with LogDNA) must be provisioned.
   - Archiving must be configured and enabled for each respective instance to the identified COS bucket.
   - The archive files visible in the specified COS bucket should match the expected LogDNA instance ID, indicating successful data archiving.

With this approach, the control will be efficiently audited, ensuring that the Activity Tracker and LogDNA logs are appropriately integrated with necessary compliance configurations.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification as Automated is correct. The control can indeed be audited using IBM Cloud APIs and CLI commands. The steps outlined leverage IBM Cloud CLI to verify the integration and configuration of Activity Tracker trails with LogDNA Logs, which is appropriate for an automated approach.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8
- The automation details are generally accurate. The use of IBM Cloud CLI commands to list service instances and check configurations is valid. However, there is a slight inconsistency in the use of CLI commands; for example, `ic cos list-buckets` should be `ibmcloud cos buckets` to align with the IBM Cloud CLI syntax.
- The program name `ibm_activity_tracker_logdna_integration_enabled` is descriptive and follows a logical naming convention, but it could be more specific about the compliance aspect being checked, such as including 'archiving_enabled'.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- Ensure consistency in CLI command syntax. For example, replace `ic cos list-buckets` with `ibmcloud cos buckets` for accuracy.
- The program name could be improved to `ibm_activity_tracker_logdna_archiving_enabled` to better reflect the specific compliance check regarding archiving.
- Consider adding a step to verify the actual data flow between Activity Tracker and LogDNA to ensure logs are being ingested correctly, not just that configurations exist.
- Include error handling or logging in the automation script to capture any issues encountered during execution.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-structured and mostly accurate. The use of IBM Cloud CLI commands is appropriate for the task, and the overall approach aligns with IBM Cloud's capabilities for automation.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Modify: Update the CLI command syntax for consistency and accuracy. Consider refining the program name for clarity. Add verification for data flow and error handling in the automation script to enhance robustness.
